coredata.dc:title: Adsorption and detection of baicalin by ZrO2 carbon microspheres modifying with boronic acid carbon dots

coredata.dc:description: The selectivity of material is crucial for adsorption and detection of target compounds from complex samples. Herein, boronic acid-containing carbon dots (BA-CDs) integration of ZrO2 carbon microspheres (named BA-CDs/ZrO2) was devised by combining the specific boron and Zr affinity towards cis-diol-containing baicalin (BAI). BA-CDs was prepared by 3-aminophenylboronic acid and adipic acid, and meanwhile Zr layer coated microspheres was synthesized by sonochemical method using poly (glycidyl methacrylate-co-ethylene glycol dimethacrylate) microspheres as hard template and deep eutectic solvents (DESs) as cross-linking agent, after carbonation, they were formed ZrO2 carbon microspheres. Then, BA-CDs were introduced into ZrO2 carbon microspheres by addition reaction. Characterization of BA-CDs/ZrO2 demonstrated that zirconium and boronic acid were successfully decorated into microspheres. The isothermal adsorption experiments determined the maximum adsorption capacity of BAI to be 49.9 mg g−1, which correlated well with the Langmuir adsorption model. The kinetic adsorption experiments further revealed that adsorption equilibrium was attained within 60 min, conforming to the pseudo-second-order adsorption model. Fluorescence experiments indicated the quenching efficiency of BAI by BA-CDs/ZrO2, and the superior fluorescence selectivity to BAI by comparing to quenching intensity of metal ions, amino acids and BAI structure like-drugs. The fluorescence detection method was established with a broader linearity in range of 0.40–300 μmol L-1 BAI concentration, 0.03 μmol L-1 of limit of detection and good reproducibility. The detection ability of BAI by BA-CDs/ZrO2 in Goji berry and Yinhuang oral liquid was verified the applicability, the measurement of BAI content was checked by HPLC method, the almost same qualitative results confirmed BA-CDs/ZrO2 practical application values.

head.ce:title: Adsorption and detection of baicalin by ZrO2 carbon microspheres modifying with boronic acid carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The selectivity of material is crucial for adsorption and detection of target compounds from complex samples. Herein, boronic acid-containing carbon dots (BA-CDs) integration of ZrO2 carbon microspheres (named BA-CDs/ZrO2) was devised by combining the specific boron and Zr affinity towards cis-diol-containing baicalin (BAI). BA-CDs was prepared by 3-aminophenylboronic acid and adipic acid, and meanwhile Zr layer coated microspheres was synthesized by sonochemical method using poly (glycidyl methacrylate-co-ethylene glycol dimethacrylate) microspheres as hard template and deep eutectic solvents (DESs) as cross-linking agent, after carbonation, they were formed ZrO2 carbon microspheres. Then, BA-CDs were introduced into ZrO2 carbon microspheres by addition reaction. Characterization of BA-CDs/ZrO2 demonstrated that zirconium and boronic acid were successfully decorated into microspheres. The isothermal adsorption experiments determined the maximum adsorption capacity of BAI to be 49.9 mg g−1, which correlated well with the Langmuir adsorption model. The kinetic adsorption experiments further revealed that adsorption equilibrium was attained within 60 min, conforming to the pseudo-second-order adsorption model. Fluorescence experiments indicated the quenching efficiency of BAI by BA-CDs/ZrO2, and the superior fluorescence selectivity to BAI by comparing to quenching intensity of metal ions, amino acids and BAI structure like-drugs. The fluorescence detection method was established with a broader linearity in range of 0.40–300 μmol L-1 BAI concentration, 0.03 μmol L-1 of limit of detection and good reproducibility. The detection ability of BAI by BA-CDs/ZrO2 in Goji berry and Yinhuang oral liquid was verified the applicability, the measurement of BAI content was checked by HPLC method, the almost same qualitative results confirmed BA-CDs/ZrO2 practical application values.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.
Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous improvement in living standards, the incidence of liver disease is on the rise. Baicalin (BAI) has significant effect on treating primary liver cancer and alleviating liver disease [1]. BAI is the flavonoid extracted from the rhizome of Scutellar iae Baicalensis [2]. It should be noted that the adsorption with high-capacity and high-selectivity is a key to obtain high-purity and high-production of BAI from herbal medicine. Besides, the latest research demonstrated only 0.74 mg mL−1 BAI is capable of inhibiting the novel coronavirus, marking it as the first inhibitor of the SARS-CoV-2 3CLpro virus [3]. As such, the way of detection of the level of BAI in clinical medicine and pharmaceutical application is required. However, because of the existence of other compounds within the biological matrix, BAI analysis is obviously interfered with, should be implemented the tedious and labor-intensive sample pretreatment to remove interferents prior to instrument analysis, i.e., liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), and even need expensive equipment [4–6]. Therefore, it is required to create a simple and high-sensitivity approach for detecting BAI. Conclusively, the adsorption and detection of BAI is of great significance to play its prominent part in medicine.

In the process of extracting BAI from herbal medicine, adsorption method has the advantages of convenient separation of solid and liquid, less solvent, etc., and has been widely used [7,8]. From consideration of obtaining the high-purity of BAI to maintaining its pharmacological activities, the selective adsorbents are critical in adsorption. For cis-diol-containing BAI, boric acid-modified materials are the most representative of the selective adsorbent [9–11], which specifically combines with cis-diol materials to form a five-membered cyclic ester based on the boronate affinity and then dissociate this ester under the alkaline/acidic conditions. And the other representative is metal oxides [12–14], for instance, TiO2 and ZrO2 microspheres have been established to exhibit adsorption capabilities for compounds containing cis-diol groups, with the adsorption by ZrO2 specifically relies on the reversible formation and dissociation of covalent complexes between ZrO2 and the cis-diol groups in alkaline or acidic environments [15]. Despite exhibiting good specificity and numerous advantages for adsorbing compounds containing cis-diol groups, there has not been any report on its application to BAI.

In terms of detection, the fluorescence method possesses highly sensitive and need no complicated sample pretreatment, having it an ideal method to analysis medicine molecules [16,17]. Apparently, fluorescence detection is also a good choice for BAI. At present, carbon dots (CDs), which are served as luminous materials, due to their excellent photoluminescence properties, good biocompatibility, and low toxicity achieved through green synthesis, they have found widespread application in fluorescence detection, and easy surface functionality since their discovery [18,19]. The fluorescence selectivity of CDs can be adjusted by the surface functionality to meet the needs of different applications [20,21]. As stated above, boronic acid-modified materials possess the high selectivity to BAI based on special boronate affinity. So, the boronic acid-containing of CDs (named BA-CDs) could have the high-sensitivity fluorescence towards BAI. Our group had done researches on BA-CDs, which were incorporated into COF to developed functional materials with fluorescence response, having the superior fluorescence properties to cis-diol compounds, such as tetracycline and mushroom toxins [22].

In this case, it is foreseeable that the combination of BA-CDs and ZrO2 miscropheres could devise dual-functional materials capable of achieving high-sensitivity fluorescence detection and selective adsorption for BAI. In this experiment, BA-CDs was prepared by 3-aminophenylboronic acid and adipic acid as precursors, and meanwhile Zr layer coated microspheres was synthesized by a simple and green sonochemical method using homemade poly (glycidyl methacrylate-co-ethylene glycol dimethacrylate) (denoted as poly (GMA-co-EDMA)) microspheres as hard template, deep eutectic solvents (DESs) as cross-linking agent and, and Zirconyl (IV) chloride octahydrate as a zirconium source, it was further carbonation to form ZrO2 carbon microspheres. Then, BA-CDs were introduced into ZrO2 carbon microspheres by addition reaction, preparing a composite BA-CDs/ZrO2. The adsorption capacity and behaviors of BA-CDs/ZrO2 were studied by isothermal and kinetic adsorption tests. The materials' fluorescence characteristics were evaluated through the application of fluorescence detection techniques, leading to the establishment of a fluorescence detection method specifically for BAI. Lastly, the measurement of BAI content in real samples was implemented by the proposed method and checked by HPLC as a comparison.

Zirconyl (IV) chloride octahydrate (ZrOCl2-8H2O), 3-aminophenylboronic acid (3-ABBA), adipic acid (AA), urea, glycidyl methacrylate (GMA), polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP), aspartic acid, leucine and serine were obtained from Damao (Tianjin, China). Azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN), ethylene dimethacrylate (EDMA), baicalin (BAI), quercetin, lutenin, gallic acid, lysine, tryptophan, tyrosine, choline chloride, quinine sulfate, ammonia and ethyl alcohol were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). The above reagents were analytical grade reagents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of blue fluorescent CDs containing boric acid groups
ce:section.ce:para: CDs containing boronic acid groups (BA-CDs) were synthesized via one step hydrothermal approach utilizing 3-ABBA and adipic acid as the precursor. Initially, 3-ABBA (0.10 g, 0.3 mmoL) and AA (0.10 g, 0.7 mmoL) were dissolved 20.0 mL of water. After that, the reaction took place in a Teflon-lined autoclave, under hydrothermal conditions at 180 °C for 6 h. The obtained product was then filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane and stored at 4 °C.

Firstly, the fabrication of DESs was referred to the literature [23]. Specifically, urea and choline chloride have been selected as the hydrogen bond donor and the hydrogen bond acceptor, respectively. The specific method was as follows: the choline chloride (0.14 g, 1 mmoL) and urea (0.12 g, 2 mmoL) were taken into a 500 mL round bottom flask, dissolved at 100 °C until clear for later to use.

Secondly, the monodisperse poly (GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres were produced by our proposed one-step seeded swelling polymerization [24]. ZrOCl-8H2O (0.30 g) was dispersed and dissolved in ethanol/water (2/1 mL, v/v), and then 0.6 mL DESs was added and stirred magnetically for 10 min, the oil-in-water emulsion system was achieved through complete homogenization using a cell disruptor. Subsequently, poly (GMA-co-EDMA) sonicated microspheres were uniformly suspended in 40 mL of deionized water and subjected to thermal agitation at 90 °C for 10 min under constant stirring conditions, and then added dropwise to the milky white solution of the above Zr source at 90 °C for 2 h. After centrifugation, the sample was washed multiple times with both ethanol and water and dried at a temperature of 60 °C.

Finally, 2.5 g ZrO2 microspheres were placed in a quartz boat, which was then situated inside a tube furnace for the purpose of carbonization. The specific step was as follows: the initial temperature was 30 °C, the heating rate was increased from 1.5 °C min−1 to 500 °C. After keeping for 4 h at 500 °C, the carbonized ZrO2 microspheres (ZrO2 carbon microspheres) were obtained after cooling at the same rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of ZrO2 carbon microspheres modified with BA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The above ZrO2 carbon microspheres were dispersed in water and 2.0 mL of BA-CDs were added. Then ammonia was utilized to adjust the pH to alkaline levels, and the mixture was stirred continuously for 12 h in a room temperature environment. After drying at 60 °C, the material of ZrO2 carbon microspheres modified with BA-CDs (named BA-CDs/ZrO2) was obtained.
The Supplementary information presents the apparatus and testing methodologies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption experiments of BA-CDs/ZrO2 to BAI
ce:section.ce:para:
The adsorption capacity (Q) for BAI was measured and analyzed through kinetic and isothermal teste. For isothermal adsorption, a 20 mg of BA-CDs/ZrO2 were added to 100 mL of BAI solutions with concentration values equal to 0.5, 1, 10, 50, 100, 200 and 300 μmol L−1, and shaken for a duration equal to 2 h at ambient temperature. This prepared samples were collected by passing the resulting solutions through a 0.22 μm membranes. The fluorescence intensity was then measured in order to calculate the concentration from the standard curve, the Q was calculated using equation
(1) :

where Q e (mg g−1) indicates the adsorption capacity at equilibrium, C 0 and C e (µmol L−1) represent the initial and equilibrium concentrations of the BAI solution, respectively, V means the volume of the BAI solution, m signifies the mass of BA-CDs/ZrO2.

The Langmuir and the Freundlich adsorption isotherm models were further employed to investigate the types of isothermal adsorption by fitting the determined isothermal adsorption data. The two models are represented by the following equations

where C e (µmol L−1) denoted the equilibrium concentration of the BAI solution, Q e (mg g−1) and Q (mg g−1) refers to the equilibrium and maximum adsorption capacities of BA-CDs/ZrO2 for BAI, respectively, K L and K F are the adsorption constant for the Langmuir and Freundlich model, respectively.

For adsorption kinetics experiment, 20 mg BA-CDs/ZrO2 adsorbent was affiliated to 100 mL BAI solution with the concentration equal to 100 µmol L−1 and oscillated at 200 r min−1 for 5, 20, 40, 60, 90, 120 and 150 min. Subsequently, the solution's fluorescence intensity was measured employing the identical techniques as those used in the isothermal adsorption experiment, and the Q values at varying time points were determined using the given equation
(1) . To investigate how Q varies over time, the dynamic adsorption process was analyzed using pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models, as represented by equations

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence experiment of BAI with BA-CDs/ZrO2
ce:section.ce:para: Material suspensions (with a concentration equal to 1.0 mg mL−1) were obtained by transmitting a given amount to BA-CDs/ZrO2 in water. Various concentrations of 3.0 mL of BAI solution, ranging in concentration from 0.4–300 μmol L−1, were combined with BA-CDs/ZrO2 suspension (2.0 mL) in a total fixed volume equaling 10.0 mL. The fluorescence spectrophotometer, set at an excitation wavelength (Ex) of 370 nm and an emission wavelength (Em) of 420 nm, was used to measure the fluorescence intensity of the prepared mixtures. After allowing the prepared solutions to stand for 20 min, the measurements were taken and this procedure was repeated three times.

Quinine sulfate was dissolved within a 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4 solution as the reference solution. The fluorescence φ of BA-CDs/ZrO2 was determined by employing quinine sulfate (φ = 55 %) as the reference substance at an Ex equal to 370 nm. The φ was calculated according to the equation
(6) :
(6)
φ =
φ R
∫ I
∫

A R
A

2

2
× 100 %
where the subscript R denotes the reference quinine sulphate solution, while φ represents the quantum yield. The symbols ∫I, A, and n denote the integral area of fluorescence intensity, absorbance, and refractive index of the solvent, respectively.

In the fluorescence selectivity experiment, a solution of 200 μmol L−1 of BAI, quercetin, gallic acid, luteolin, lysine, tryptophan, aspartic acid, leucine, tyrosine and serine were prepared. Additionally, twelve metal ions (Zr4+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Pd2+, Na+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Li+, Co2+, K+, Cd2+, Cu2+) were respectively mixed with 1.0 mg mL−1 of BA-CDs/ZrO2 solution (1:1, v:v). After diluting to 10.0 mL, the fluorescence intensity of BA-CDs/ZrO2 at the Ex of 370 nm was measured. Furthermore, the solution containing metal ions, drug molecules, and amino acids was combined with the BAI and BA-CDs/ZrO2 mixtures and subsequently diluted by adding ultra-pure water to obtain final volume equal to 10.0 mL. To assess the selectivity of the BA-CDs/ZrO2 sample, the variations in fluorescence intensity were documented with Ex and Em set at 370 nm and 420 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of BAI in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of BA-CDs/ZrO2 for determining BAI was applied to analyze Goji berry and Yinhuang oral liquid purchased from a local pharmacy in Yinchuan. For the Yinhuang oral liquid, its concentration was diluted 100-fold within the linear range. The content of BAI in both Goji berry and Yinhuang oral liquid was then determined by adding 1.0 mg mL−1 BA-CDs/ZrO2 under the optimized conditions (pH 9.5, Ex 370 nm and Em 420 nm). Subsequently, the standard addition method was employed for further analysis, with spike concentrations set at 5, 10, and 15 μmol L-1. The recovery rates were determined using the measured alterations in fluorescence intensity as the basis.

Meanwhile, the real samples, Goji berry and Yinhuang oral liquid, were subjected to quantitative analysis by HPLC as controls using the same treatment method for comparative purposes. The following were the chromatographic conditions: a C18 column (250 × 4.6 mm, 5 µm), mobile phase consisting of methanol and 0.4 % phosphoric acid aqueous solution (52:48, v/v), injection volume of 20 μL, detection wavelength set at 278 nm, flow rate of 1.0 mL min−1, and column temperature maintained at 30 ℃.

Research has shown that the adsorption of cis-diol compounds onto ZrO2 occurs through a reversible process involving the formation and dissociation of covalent complexes between the cis-diol compounds and ZrO2. While that by boronic acid-containing adsorbents is based on reversible cyclic boronic esters of formation/dissociation between boronic acid and cis-diol compounds in acidic or alkaline aqueous solutions [25,26].The target of this paper is to combine the feature of ZrO2 and boronic acid-containing material to develop a new adsorbent, which has high selectivity for BAI bearing cis-diol groups. In this study, ZrO2-derived poly (GMA-co-EDMA) carbon microspheres and boronic acid-containing CDs (BA-CDs) were used to achieve this purpose. The synthesis process of BA-CDs/ZrO2 is illustrated in Fig. 1
A. First, as stated in our earlier report [27], spherical poly (GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres were synthesized in a straightforward manner through one-step seeded swelling polymerization. Subsequently, these poly (GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres served as templates. DESs which were synthesized at ambient temperature through the utilization of choline chloride and urea as precursors, were used as crosslinker, and ZrOCl2-8H2O was employed as zirconium source, the Zr layer was polymerized through hydrogen bonds and other interaction, and wrapped by DESs around the poly (GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres surface. Then, the resulting Zr-derived microsphere templated by poly (GMA-co-EDMA) was carbonized at 500 °C, yielding ZrO2 carbon microspheres. Next, BA-CDs were synthesized utilizing 3-ABBA and adipic acid as precursors, under alkaline conditions, the facile modification of BA-CDs onto the ZrO2 carbon microspheres occurred through a reaction between the double bond in carbon microsphere and amino group in BA-CDs, thereby forming a composite microsphere consisting of BA-CDs and ZrO2 (denoted BA-CDs/ZrO2). Here, BA-CDs were introduced into ZrO2 microspheres to promote adsorption capacity and selectivity towards BAI, as well as concurrently enabling fluorescence detection (Fig. 1 B).

The SEM observations indicated spherical structures in the synthesized poly (GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres, averaging around 5.0 μm in diameter. (Fig. 2
A and B), After Zr layer wrapped around the poly (GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres surface, as shown in Fig. 2 C and D, the resulting Zr-derived microspheres remained spherical with a smoother surface, their average size increased to 6.1 μm, obviously larger than the pristine poly (GMA-co-EDMA). The SEM of BA-CDs is displayed in Fig. S1, it had an average particle size of approximately 3.13 nm and was spherical in shape. After carbonation of Zr-derived microspheres and then bonding BA-CDs, the particle size of BA-CDs/ZrO2 composite microsphere dramatically shrunk to about 2.0 μm (Fig. 2 E and F). Additionally, the mapping images of BA-CDs/ZrO2 are given in Fig. 2 G, H and I, the key elements of C, Zr and B were all observed, demonstrating the successful Zr coated and BA-CDs modification onto poly (GMA-co-EDMA). Additionally, BA-CDs/ZrO2 composite microspheres underwent further characterized via XRD, as illustrated in Fig. S2. The peaks observed in the diffraction pattern at angles of 30.1°, 34.59°, 50.2°, and 59.7° correspond to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) crystallographic planes of ZrO2, respectively, indicating successful deposition of ZrO2 onto the microsphere surfaces.

The specific surface area of poly (GMA-co-EDMA), Zr microspheres, and the BA-CDs/ZrO2 were analyzed via N2 adsorption–desorption experiments, and the results of which are presented in Fig. S3A and B. It was noted that the quantity of N2 adsorbed remained fairly stable when the relative pressure was less than 0.8. However, a sharp increase in N2 adsorption was observed as its value exceeded 0.8, there was a dramatic increase in nitrogen adsorption. This indicated that the three materials contained abundant macropores, which facilitated higher mass transfer rates. The specific surface area and other data of the three materials are given in Table S1. The specific surface area increased from 16.86 m2 g−1 of poly (GMA-co-EDMA) to 29.22 m2 g−1 of Zr microspheres, but after carbonization and modifying with BA-CDs, that of BA-CDs/ZrO2 composite microspheres was decreased to 19.66 m2 g−1, and the pore volume of those materials was over than 0.04 cm3 g−1. Additionally, the pore size distribution was significantly decreased after progressive modification (Fig. S3B), BA-CDs/ZrO2 had about 8.3 nm of average pore size, which could be help sustain the mass transfer rate during adsorption process.

Furthermore, the elements composition of synthesized micropheres was characterized by high-resolution XPS, as shown in Fig. S3C. The peaks matching to C 1 s at 284.6 eV and O 1 s at 532.2 eV arose in the XPS spectrum of poly (GMA-co-EDMA). Upon Zr coating, the Zr 3d and Zr 3p peaks appeared at 181.7 eV and 332.3 eV in Zr microspheres. After further the modification of BA-CDs, the intensity of C 1 s significantly increased and a prominent B 3d peak at 191.9 eV was observed in BA-CDs/ZrO2 composite microspheres. Subsequently, the functional groups present in poly (GMA-co-EDMA), Zr microspheres, and BA-CDs/ZrO2 were measured by FT-IR spectroscopy, and their respective FT-IR spectra are presented in Fig. S3D. In the spectra of pristine poly (GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres, at 910 cm−1, a characteristic peak corresponding to the epoxy group was observed, whereas this peak was not present in the spectra of the Zr microspheres. it’s shown that the function of Zr layer is promoted by protonation of epoxy group. Meanwhile, the characteristic peak at 667 cm−1 was the antisymmetric stretching vibration of Zr-O-Zr bonds, indicating the successful preparation of Zr microspheres. In BA-CDs/ZrO2 spectrum, prominent peaks were observed at 1637 cm−1 and 3417 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of C=C and the absorption peak of –NH2, respectively. And the C=O peak disappeared at 1729 cm−1, whereas the characteristic peak for Zr-O-Zr bonds at 667 cm−1 still showed. Those changes could be probably ascribed the implementation of carbonation process. Besides, new peaks of B-C and B-O bonds emerged at 576 cm−1 and 1394 cm−1 in BA-CDs/ZrO2, respectively, proving the successful introduction of BA-CDs into ZrO2 microspheres. Conclusively, those results confirm the triumphant synthesis of BA-CDs/ZrO2.

The Q is a crucial indicator for assessing the adsorption performance of materials. This study assessed the adsorption of BAI by BA-CDs/ZrO2 using isotherm experiments, with ZrO2 carbon microspheres as a control to highlight the enhancement from BA-CDs in adsorption. The isotherm adsorption curves of BA-CDs and ZrO2 carbon microspheres are presented in Fig. 3
A, upon initiating the adsorption process, there was a swift rise in the BAI concentration, specifically within the range of 0 to 100 μmol L-1, after which an adsorption equilibrium was attained at 100 μmol L-1. Although the change in adsorption behavior of BA-CDs/ZrO2 in the range of testing concentrations was consistent with that ZrO2 carbon microspheres, the maximum Q of BA-CDs/ZrO2 for BAI was 49.9 mg g−1, which was nearly double that of ZrO2 carbon microspheres (27.5 mg g−1). This could be attributed the introduction of BA-CDs in BA-CDs/ZrO2, which have the special boron affinity to BAI, could recognize BAI more efficiently and rapidly compared to ZrO2 carbon micropheres that lack BA-CDs, resulting in superior adsorption capacity. Next, the adsorption mechanism of BAI by BA-CDs/ZrO2 was analyzed using isothermal adsorption models of Langmuir equation (2) and Freundlich equation (3) . As illustrated in Fig. 3 B and C, it could be seen that Langmuir model (the linear correlation coefficient, R = 0.9912 and 0.9929 for BA-CDs/ZrO2 and ZrO2 carbon microspheres) was more appropriate than Freundlich model (R = 0.9818 and 0.9860 for BA-CDs/ZrO2 and ZrO2 carbon microspheres). Notably, both BA-CDs/ZrO2 and ZrO2 carbon microspheres demonstrated a stronger agreement with Langmuir model in term of BAI adsorption mechanism, this indicating a monolayer adsorption pattern on a uniform surface, with the highest possible adsorption capacity.

The adsorption rate is considered one of the most important parameters for analyzing the adsorption kinetics test. As illustrated in Fig. 3 D, the kinetic adsorption curves obtained for the BA-CDs/ZrO2 and the ZrO2 carbon microspheres indicated that the adsorption of BAI by both materials increased significantly within 60 min, finally reaching a plateau. The saturation adsorption of BAI by BA-CDs/ZrO2 was equal to 52.9 mg g−1, which was still significantly more excellent compared to that of the ZrO2 carbon micropheres (29.96 mg g−1). Moreover, this study also examined the mechanism underlying adsorption kinetics. Pseudo-first-order (5) and pseudo-second-order (6) kinetic model equations were employed to fit the results of kinetic adsorption. It can be seen from Fig. 3 E and F, the R (0.9913 for BA-CDs/ZrO2 and 0.9942 for ZrO2 carbon micropheres) of pseudo-second-order model was higher than that for pseudo-first-order model (0.8061 for BA-CDs/ZrO2 and 0.9121 for ZrO2 carbon micropheres). By comparing two kinetic model, the materials were more suitable for the pseudo-second-order model, demonstrating chemical adsorption of BAI on BA-CDs/ZrO2.

The adsorption capacities of BAI by BA-CDs/ZrO2 was assessed by a comparison with several adsorbents. It could be found that Q of BA-CDs/ZrO2 was superior than that of a collagen fiber adsorbent using glutaraldehyde as a cross-linking agent (Q m = 1.82 mg g−1) [28], molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) types, such as, MIPs with a ternary DESs as functional monomer (Q m = 7.06 mg g−1) [29], MMIPs synthesized by precipitation polymerization (Q m = 2.59 × 10-4 mg g−1) [30] and SBA-15 as a substrate of MIP (2.65 × 10-4 mg g−1) [31], and recently reported using a food grade methyl cinnamate for preparation of BAI adsorbent (Q m = 21.1 mg g−1) [32]. Conclusively, Q of BA-CDs/ZrO2 on BAI (49.9 mg g−1) were remarkably more above than that of above materials. The adsorption result indicated that, which integrated of ZrO2 and BA-CDs to provide the more active site and special affinity for recognizing BAI, is assuredly promote Q for BAI.

In addition, the reusability performance of BA-CDs/ZrO2 was evaluated by being treated with methanol, acid and base. As illustrated in Fig. S4, some decline in adsorption capacity was observed after five consecutive adsorption–desorption cycles, but BA-CDs/ZrO still maintained exceptional stability with > 95 % Q in the fifth regeneration cycle by comparing the first adsorption efficiency. These findings confirmed the superior reutilization of BA-CDs/ZrO2 in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence properties in detection of BAI using BA-CDs/ZrO2
ce:section.ce:para:
Considering that BA-CDs/ZrO2 materials were decorated with luminescent BA-CDs, their fluorescence properties were studied using a fluorescence spectrometer, with a 1 mg mL−1 of BA-CDs/ZrO2 suspension serving as test solution. Here, the BA-CDs/ZrO2 suspension appeared transparent with light yellow color (left) under visible light and fluoresces bright blue under the 365 nm ultraviolet lamp (right) (see inserted photos in Fig. 4
A). As depicted in Fig. 4 A, when the Em reached 420 nm, the BA-CDs/ZrO2 suspension displayed an increase in fluorescence intensity within the Ex at range of 340–370 nm and decreased beyond 370 nm. So, in the following fluorescence experiments, an optimal fluorescence response was achieved at an Ex of 370 nm and an Em at 420 nm. Besides, it should be noted that the narrow fluorescence interval indicated the suitability of BA-CDs/ZrO2 is well-suited for precise fluorescence determination. Then, the fluorescence intensity of BA-CDs/ZrO2 suspension and ZrO2 carbon microspheres at the Em of 420 nm was observed at 370 Ex as shown in Fig. 4 B, the fluorescence intensity of BA-CDs/ZrO2 (5600) was almost 5 times higher than that of ZrO2 carbon micropheres (1100), manifesting the BA-CDs role in fluorescence response. When adding BAI under alkaline condition, it could be observed that BA-CDs/ZrO2 showed the significant fluorescence quenching effect for BAI (67 % of quenching intensity). Whereas for ZrO2 carbon micropheres, it did not occur obvious fluorescence quenching (Fig. 4 B).

Several processes can account for the fluorescence quenching mechanism within the CDs, such as the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photo-induced transfer of electrons, static quenching, dynamic quenching, and the internal filter effect [33,34]. In this study, a comparison of the UV absorption spectra obtained for the BAI with the excitation and emission spectra of BA-CDs/ZrO2, no overlapping spectral lines was observed (Fig. 4 C), indicating that the internal filter effect required for fluorescence quenching theory was not satisfied [35]. The FRET theory describes the phenomenon where energy transfer occurs between a donor fluorophore (BA-CDs/ZrO2) and an acceptor fluorophore (BAI), leading to a fluorescence emission peak from the BAI at the Ex of the BA-CDs/ZrO2. The fluorescence emission peak is caused by the fluorescence quenching effect of the light emitted by the absorbing material [36]. In fact, in the present fluorescence test system, no BAI fluorescence emission peak was detected at the Ex of BA-CDs/ZrO2 (Fig. 4 C), which directly ruled out FRET as a potential mechanism.

In the different BAI concentration range, a linear Stern-Volmer relationship between changes of fluorescence intensity and BAI concentration was observed (Fig. S5), and obtained the quenching constant (KSV) of 1.4 × 10-2 M. At the same time, the fluorescence lifetime of BA-CDs/ZrO2 at 420 nm Em and the same BAI concentration was determined the effect of BAI. As illustrated in Fig. 4 D, the fluorescence lifetime changed from 4.08 ns to 3.88 ns upon interaction with BAI. The almost stable fluorescence lifetime inferred the static quenching mode is likely responsible for this fluorescence response.

The static quenching always occurs when the detection target is unable to move or change its conformation in response to an external stimulus [37]. So, maybe the electron transfer process could account for fluorescence quenching of BA-CDs/ZrO2 toward BAI. According to previous report [38], BAI has an electron aromatic ring and cis-diol, resulting in a strong charge transfer to an electron-deficient group. Since BA-CDs/ZrO2 possessed abundant active groups, such as boronic acid, phenyl, ZrO2 groups, when BA-CDs/ZrO2 suspension adding BAI, BA-CDs in BA-CDs/ZrO2 have the ability to capture cis-diol-containing BAI through the formation of boronic ester bonds via an esterification reaction, having the strong charge transfer in the formation of boron affinity forces and making occurrence of fluorescence quenching. Moreover, although ZrO2 carbon microspheres lacked luminous BA-CDs, a weak fluorescence emission peak at 400 nm was also observed. After adding BAI, weak fluorescence quenching occurred (Fig. 4 B). This is because the unsaturated Zr (IV) acts as a Lewis acid, providing coordination tendencies toward the cis-diol groups [15]. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching to BAI ascribed to the coordination reaction where bonding electrons was from the cis-diol in BAI and Zr in ZrO2 carbon micropheres, having the charge transfer to induce quenching phenomenon. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of BAI by ZrO2 carbon micropheres was compared with that by BA-CDs/ZrO2, the quenching intensity by BA-CDs/ZrO2 was obviously stronger (Fig. 4 B), indicating that the synergistic effect of coordination interaction by ZrO2 and boron affinity forces by BA-CDs constitutes an effective strategy for capturing BAI, resulting in the strong charge transfer, thereby having the strong fluorescence quenching. In principle, under alkaline condition, BA-CDs and ZrO2 could imparted the boron affinity and coordination force to BAI. Therefore, the fluorescence response should be pH dependent. Indeed, as presented in Fig. 4 E, no fluorescence quenching was observed in acidic solution, whereas fluorescence intensity displayed a substantial decline at over 7.0 of pH solution. So in fluorescence experiments, pH of 9.5 was selected to optimize the BAI response. Besides, because of synthesized BA-CDs using 3-aminophenylboronic acid as precursor, the devised BA-CDs/ZrO2 possesses abundant amino groups. The aromatic ring of BAI was electron-rich and exhibits robust charge transfer to the amino group, which was electron-deficient. As illustrated in Fig. 4 F, the FT-IR spectra of BA-CDs/ZrO2 was found the –NH2 of absorption peak at 3417 cm−1, while that of BAI was also observed the C=C peak derived from aromatic ring at 1573 cm−1. After the adsorption of BAI by BA-CDs/ZrO2, the intense charge transfer interaction arises between the aromatic ring, which is electron-rich, and the amino group, which is electron-deficient, led to the near disappearance of the corresponding peaks at 3417 cm−1 and 1573 cm−1, simultaneously enhancing the fluorescence quenching of BA-CDs/ZrO2. Furthermore, a new characteristic peak of ester bond at 1159 cm−1 was appeared in the FT-IR of BA-CDs/ZrO2-BAI mixture system, confirming the formation of five-membered cyclic ester by boron affinity forces between boric acid in BA-CDs and cis-diol in BAI. Since BA-CDs and ZrO2 could imparted the boron affinity and coordination force to BAI after adsorption, the obvious augmented peaks at 1351 cm−1 and 1571 cm−1 were attributed the bending variation of B-O-C and Zr-O–H of the formation of five-membered rings between BA-CDs/ZrO2 and BAI. Apparently, the devised BA-CDs/ZrO2 had the superior fluorescence quenching of BAI, facilitating the good detection and adsorption of BAI from complex samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence selectivity of BA-CDs/ZrO2 for BAI
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence specificity of BA-CDs/ZrO2 should be investigated to cope with the complex detection system. In this systems, BA-CDs/ZrO2 suspension was respectively dispersed into solutions of catechol with cis-diol and resorcinol without cis-diol at the same concentration, and their fluorescence response are displayed in Fig. 5
A. It could be seen that BA-CDs/ZrO2 showed fluorescence quenching for catechol, whereas no fluorescence changed for resorcinol by comparing pristine fluorescence intensity of BA-CDs/ZrO2. This is because catechol has the cis-diol in its chemical structure, which enables the boron affinity interaction to BA-CDs/ZrO2, resulting in the fluorescence quenching. It should be noted although BAI and catechol both contain cis-diol, BA-CDs/ZrO2 implemented fluorescence quenching for BAI (quenching intensity of 67 %) was more obvious than that of catechol (quenching intensity of 48 %), indicating that the fabricated BA-CDs/ZrO2 had the special fluorescence quenching for cis-diol-containing compounds, but the quenching degree to BAI had the upper hand. Thereafter, the fluorescence selectivity of BA-CDs/ZrO2 for BAI structure-like drug molecules and various types of potential interfering substances such metal ions and amino acids, which could a high chance of existence in biological and drug systems, was further investigated. At the same concentration of 1.0 mmol L-1 for each test substance and compared with the fluorescence intensity of pristine BA-CDs/ZrO2, BA-CDs/ZrO2 exhibited minimal fluorescence quenching for BAI-like drug molecules (quercetin, luteolin and gallic acid) and seven amino acids (lysine, tryptophan, tyrosine, aspartic acid, leucine and serine) (Fig. 5 B). Plainly, the quenching of BAI-like drug molecules and amino acids by BA-CDs/ZrO2 was negligible by comparing to that of BAI. Whereas for metal ions, BA-CDs/ZrO2 had the any fluorescence response (Fig. 5 C), since there were no interaction sites between the metal ions and BA-CDs/ZrO2 to induce fluorescence response. In practice, the testing real samples should be a sophisticated system comprising diverse types of compounds. Therefore, the next experiment investigated the fluorescence selectivity by using a mixed solution containing BAI, metal ions (Na+ and Zn2+), BAI-like drugs (quercetin and gallic acid), and amino acids (tryptophan and leucine), the result is showed in Fig. 5 D. It could be observed that the fluorescence quenching of this mixed solution by BA-CDs/ZrO2 also occurred by comparing to that of pristine BA-CDs/ZrO2, and the quenching intensity for mixture solution (64 %) was almost same to that for only BAI solution (67 %). This demonstrated that BA-CDs/ZrO2 possessed superior recognition capacity to BAI even in existing of various types of interfering substances. Conclusively, the good fluorescence selectivity of BA-CDs/ZrO2 to BAI could speculate its potential for determining BAI in complex samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantification of BAI by BA-CDs/ZrO2
ce:section.ce:para:
As described above, the fluorescence quenching of BAI by BA-CDs/ZrO2 was demonstrated. When determining BAI by BA-CDs/ZrO2, a linear relationship should be established between the fluorescence intensity of BA-CDs/ZrO2 and varying concentrations of BAI. As depicted in Fig. 6
A, the concentration of BAI rose, the fluorescence intensity of BA-CDs/ZrO2 decreased steadily, exhibiting a significant linear relationship with the ratio of fluorescence change, F/F 0 (F is the fluorescence intensity of BA-CDs/ZrO2 after quenching by BAI, F 0 is the pristine fluorescence intensity of BA-CDs/ZrO2) and BAI concentration in the range of 0.4–300 μmol L-1. The resulting linear relationship was listed in Fig. 6 B, clearly, the fluorescence quenching process was categorized into two separate phases. During the initial phase, the fluorescence quenching occurred as BAI concentration increased from 0.4 to 1.0 μmol L-1, the adaptation equation for BA-CDs/ZrO2 was F/F 0 = 0.6093 [C] + 1.0349 and its R = 0.9944. At the second stage, a linear relationship between F/F 0 and BAI concentration in the range of 1–300 μmol L-1 was maintained, having a linear equation, F/F 0 = 7.8029 [C] + 0.4353 and its R = 0.9939. These results indicated a two-phase model and good linear regression coefficients. The quenching phenomena might speculate that BA-CDs/ZrO2 had two types of interaction sites, such as BA-CDs and ZrO2, to adsorb BAI. At low BAI concentration, since BA-CDs/ZrO2 had a substantial number of fluorescence active groups, just only BA-CDs or ZrO2 provided the sufficient sites to response BAI, resulting in the quick fluorescence quenching. As progressive increasing the BAI concentration, the active groups in both ZrO2 and BA-CDs for interaction with BAI might simultaneously response to make quenching, having another good relationship between F/F 0 and BAI concentration. Furthermore, the low limit of detection (LOD) was calculated via the 3σ rule (σ = S 0/S, here, S 0 represents the standard deviation calculated from seven blank measurements, whereas S signifies the slope of the calibration curve), and the LOD in fluorescence detection of BAI was found to be 0.03 μmol L-1. Overall, the good linear relationship and low LOD proved the potential in fluorescence determination of BAI using BA-CDs/ZrO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation method for BAI determination in real samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The following experiment was conducted to investigate the practical application of the BA-CDs/ZrO2 for detecting the presence of BAI within the Goji berry and Yinhuang oral liquid. The fluorescence quenching of the Goji berry and Yinhuang oral liquid by BA-CDs/ZrO2 was observed under an Ex equal to 370 nm upon an Em equaling 420 nm and a pH of 9.5 as the test solution. The data obtained from this characterization is illustrated in Fig. 7
A and B, which demonstrate the existence of BAI in these samples. Based on the sample dilution multiple and standard curve, the measured content BAI within the Goji berry solution was equal to 9.08 mg g−1, while its value was 24.93 mg mL−1 in the Yinhuang oral liquid. The contents of BAI in the Goji berry and Yinhuang oral liquid were verified via HPLC measurements to assess the accuracy of its measurement. As shown in Fig. 7 C and D, the chromatographic peaks of BAI in Goji berry and Yinhuang oral liquid were found by comparing to the standard BAI, and the concentration of BAI in Goji berry was 8.96 mg g−1 and 24.96 mg mL−1 in Yinhuang oral liquid. Obviously, our established fluorescence method for BAI quantification had the same level as HPLC, proving its excellent ability to measure BAI in real samples.

The test reproducibility and stability by BA-CDs/ZrO2 was tested by the spiked recovery. As illustrated in Fig. S6, when spiking 5, 10 and 15 μmol L-1 of BAI to Yinhuang oral liquid, a significant fluorescence quenching was all found after addition of BA-CDs/ZrO2 suspension, and the resulting recovery rates were 98.73–100.59 % with relative standard deviation of 0.21–0.41 % (Table S2), proving the reproducibility and stability of fluorescence detection by BA-CDs/ZrO2.

Apart from fluorescence method, in the detection of BAI, the other methods, such as LC-MS/MS, HPLC, UPLC and electrochemical, etc., have been applied until now, as summarized in Table 1 [16,38–45]. Notably, compared to those reports, our proposed method had the following advantages. ①The superior salience is manifest in a broader linear range for our method, this performance is good in measurement of samples with different unknown BAI concentration. ②Among them, our method had the lowest LOD, greatly meeting the current requirements of trace sample analysis. ③Generally, for the analysis BAI in real samples, it should be noted LC-MS/MS and HPLC had the main problem in complicated sample treatment and tedious monitoring by professionals, and high-cost instrumentation, whereas for our method, its sample treatment, detection procedure and testing cost were relatively simple, quick and low. Besides above, although the electrochemical method using phosphorus co-doped hollow porous carbon microspheres as probe had a similar level of LOD (0.043 μmol L-1) as our method (0.03 μmol L-1), it consistently exhibits poor reproducibility [46]. Recently, our team developed an anti-fouling fluorescence probe by encapsulating boric acid type CDs within UiO-66-NH2 and introducing the zwitterionic polymers as anti-fouling sits on the external, resulting in a lower LOD of 0.0064 μmol L-1 [38]. Although LOD of fluorescence detection of BAI by BA-CDs/ZrO2 was higher than that of our previously reported anti-fouling fluorescence probe, it should be noted this probe of BA-CDs/ZrO2 possessed simple synthesis, and the established method exhibited a broader linear range by comparing to the previous work with 0.01–0.08 μmol L-1 linear, highlighting the superiority of fluorescence method by BA-CDs/ZrO2. Therefore, comparison results between our study and the recently reported works manifests the significant promising potential in fluorescence detection of BAI by BA-CDs/ZrO2.

In this experiment, poly (GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres serving as a hard template, the Zr layer was self-polymerized on poly (GMA-co-EDMA) surface using DESs as cross-linking agent, and then ZrO2 carbon microspheres were obtained by carbonation. BA-CDs containing boric acids were incorporated into the ZrO2 carbon microsphere to successfully synthesize BA-CDs/ZrO2 composite microspheres. BA-CDs/ZrO2 possessed some advantages. Firstly, the adsorption performance of BA-CDs/ZrO2 was 49.9 mg g−1 for BAI, far superior to the reported studies. Secondly, BA-CDs/ZrO2 with blue fluorescence exhibited the superior selectivity to BAI by comparing to the fluorescence response intensity to metal ions, amino acids and BAI structure-like drugs. So, BA-CDs/ZrO2 was capable of fluorescence detection of BAI, and it is observed that the fluorescence intensity exhibited a linear relationship with the BAI concentration in a wide range (0.40–300 μmol L-1), the low LOD of 0.03 μmol L-1 and the good detection reproducibility. Lastly, the detection ability of BAI by BA-CDs/ZrO2 in Goji berry and Yinhuang oral liquid was verified the applicability, the measurement of BAI content was checked by HPLC method, the almost same qualitative results confirmed BA-CDs/ZrO2 practical application values. In summary, BA-CDs/ZrO2 combined the specific boron affinity, the high affinity of Zr with the fluorescence properties of CDs, allows for the simultaneous adsorption and fluorescence detection of cis-diol compounds. It is estimated that our devised materials could offer potentials in fields such as drug separation and purification as well as monitoring.
